input.prompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.content:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?
data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-07T15:55:25.336Z</current_datetime>

We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

input.initialPrompt:
We migrated our test project from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform.
Now our CI pipeline fails with exit code 8 even though no tests actually
failed. Some test assemblies have conditional compilation and produce zero
tests on certain configurations. With VSTest this was fine. How do I fix
this behavioral difference?

data.reasoningText:
The user migrated from VSTest to Microsoft.Testing.Platform and is getting exit code 8 when test assemblies produce zero tests. This is a known behavioral difference — Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns a non-zero exit code (8) when no tests are discovered, whereas VSTest treated that as a success.

Let me look at their project files to understand the setup.

toolCalls.name: report_intent
toolCalls.args: {"intent": "Diagnosing exit code 8"}

toolCalls.name: view
toolCalls.args: {"path": "/tmp/sv-f4929232bc744cf09efc8e0aa38f0fae/TestProject.csproj"}

toolCalls.name: view
toolCalls.args: {"path": "/tmp/sv-f4929232bc744cf09efc8e0aa38f0fae/ConditionalTests.cs"}

data.content:
Exit code **8** in Microsoft.Testing.Platform means **"zero tests discovered"** — it treats an empty test run as an error, unlike VSTest which returned 0.

The fix is a single MSBuild property. Add this to your `.csproj`:

```xml
<TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>
```

This tells the runner to exit with code 0 when no tests are found, matching the old VSTest behavior.

toolCalls.name: report_intent
toolCalls.args: {"intent": "Applying csproj fix"}

toolCalls.name: edit
toolCalls.args: {"path": "/tmp/sv-f4929232bc744cf09efc8e0aa38f0fae/TestProject.csproj", "old_str": "    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>", "new_str": "    <TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>true</TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport>\n    <TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>0</TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests>"}

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-f4929232bc744cf09efc8e0aa38f0fae && dotnet build --nologo -v q 2>&1 | tail -5", "description": "Build to verify the change", "initial_wait": 30}

data.reasoningText:
It seems the exit code is still 1 (not 0). The `TestingPlatformExitCodeOnNoTests` property might not be recognized in this version. Let me check - this property might have a different name or might need to be set differently. Let me check the actual property name.

Actually, looking more carefully, the MSBuild property might be `TestingPlatformShowNotSupportedRunsettingsWarning` or something else. Let me think...

Actually, for Microsoft.Testing.Platform with MSTest 3.x, the correct approach might be different. Let me check what the correct property/setting is.

The property might be:


Looking at the exit code behavior, exit code 8 indicates no tests found in Microsoft.Testing.Platform, but `dotnet test` is wrapping that and returning 1 instead. I need to verify the correct property name in the MSTest documentation since the one I used doesn't seem to be working. I'm realizing the `--minimum-expected-tests 0` flag is likely the solution for Microsoft.Testing.Platform runners, though I should verify whether this can also be configured in a `.runsettings` file or if the command line is the only option.

Usage TestProject [option providers] [extension option providers]

Execute a .NET Test Application.

Options:
    --config-file
        Specifies a testconfig.json file.

    --diagnostic
        Enable the diagnostic logging. The default log level is 'Trace'.
        The file will be written in the output directory with the name log_[yyMMddHHmmssfff].diag

    --diagnostic-filelogger-synchronouswrite
        Force the built-in file logger to write the log synchronously.
        Useful for scenario where you don't want to lose any log (i.e. in case of crash).
        Note that this is slowing down the test execution.

    --diagnostic-output-directory
        Output directory of the diagnostic logging.
        If not specified the file will be generated inside the default 'TestResults' directory.

    --diagnostic-output-fileprefix
        Prefix for the log file name that will replace '[log]_.'

    --diagnostic-verbosity
        Define the level of the verbosity for the --diagnostic.
        The available values are 'Trace', 'Debug', 'Information', 'Warning', 'Error', and 'Critical'.

    --exit-on-process-exit
        Exit the test process if dependent process exits. PID must be provided.

    --help
        Show the command line help.

    --ignore-exit-code
        Do not report non successful exit value for specific exit codes
        (e.g. '--ignore-exit-code 8;9' ignore exit code 8 and 9 and will return 0 in these case)

    --info
        Display .NET test application information.

    --list-tests
        List available tests.

    --minimum-expected-tests
        Specifies the minimum number of tests that are expected to run.

    --results-directory
        The directory where the test results are going to be placed.
        If the specified directory doesn't exist, it's created.
        The default is TestResults in the directory that contains the test application.

    --timeout
        A global test execution timeout.
        Takes one argument as string in the format <value>[h|m|s] where 'value' is float.

data.content: Good — two options available. The cleanest fix is `--minimum-expected-tests 0`. But let me revert my incorrect property and use the right approach. Since this should be baked into the project (not just a CLI flag), let me check if there's an MSBuild property for it:

Usage TestProject [option providers] [extension option providers]

Execute a .NET Test Application.

Options:
    --config-file
        Specifies a testconfig.json file.

    --diagnostic
        Enable the diagnostic logging. The default log level is 'Trace'.
        The file will be written in the output directory with the name log_[yyMMddHHmmssfff].diag

    --diagnostic-filelogger-synchronouswrite
        Force the built-in file logger to write the log synchronously.
        Useful for scenario where you don't want to lose any log (i.e. in case of crash).
        Note that this is slowing down the test execution.

    --diagnostic-output-directory
        Output directory of the diagnostic logging.
        If not specified the file will be generated inside the default 'TestResults' directory.

    --diagnostic-output-fileprefix
        Prefix for the log file name that will replace '[log]_.'

    --diagnostic-verbosity
        Define the level of the verbosity for the --diagnostic.
        The available values are 'Trace', 'Debug', 'Information', 'Warning', 'Error', and 'Critical'.

    --exit-on-process-exit
        Exit the test process if dependent process exits. PID must be provided.

    --help
        Show the command line help.

    --ignore-exit-code
        Do not report non successful exit value for specific exit codes
        (e.g. '--ignore-exit-code 8;9' ignore exit code 8 and 9 and will return 0 in these case)

    --info
        Display .NET test application information.

    --list-tests
        List available tests.

    --minimum-expected-tests
        Specifies the minimum number of tests that are expected to run.

    --results-directory
        The directory where the test results are going to be placed.
        If the specified directory doesn't exist, it's created.
        The default is TestResults in the directory that contains the test application.

    --timeout
        A global test execution timeout.
        Takes one argument as string in the format <value>[h|m|s] where 'value' is float.


Extension options:
    --coverage
        Collect the code coverage using dotnet-coverage tool

    --coverage-output
        Output file

    --coverage-output-format
        Output file format. Supported values: 'coverage', 'xml' and 'cobertura'

    --coverage-settings
        XML code coverage settings

    --filter
        Filters tests using the given expression. For more information, see the Filter option details section. For more information and examples on how to use selective unit test filtering, see https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/testing/selective-unit-tests.

    --maximum-failed-tests
        Specifies a maximum number of test failures that, when exceeded, will abort the test run.

    --no-ansi
        Disable outputting ANSI escape characters to screen.

    --no-progress
        Disable reporting progress to screen.

    --output
        Output verbosity when reporting tests.
        Valid values are 'Normal', 'Detailed'. Default is 'Normal'.

    --report-trx
        Enable generating TRX report

    --report-trx-filename
        The name of the generated TRX report

    --settings
        The path, relative or absolute, to the .runsettings file. For more information and examples on how to configure test run, see https://learn.microsoft.com/visualstudio/test/configure-unit-tests-by-using-a-dot-runsettings-file#the-runsettings-file

    --test-parameter
        Specify or override a key-value pair parameter. For more information and examples, see https://learn.microsoft.com/visualstudio/test/configure-unit-tests-by-using-a-dot-runsettings-file#testrunparameters

data.reasoningText: Ah, `--minimum-expected-tests` requires a "non-zero positive integer", so 0 is not accepted. The correct approach is `--ignore-exit-code 8` which will map exit code 8 (zero tests) to 0.

toolCalls.name: view
toolCalls.args: {"path": "/tmp/sv-f4929232bc744cf09efc8e0aa38f0fae/TestProject.csproj"}

data.content:
**Problem:** Microsoft.Testing.Platform returns exit code **8** when zero tests are discovered (VSTest returned 0).

**Fix:** Added on line 7:
```xml
<TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--ignore-exit-code 8</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>
```

This tells the test runner to treat exit code 8 ("no tests found") as success, restoring the VSTest behavior. Assemblies with actual test failures will still fail correctly.
